items.myth: Cross-validation replaces a test set entirely.
items.reality: CV improves development-time estimates and model comparison; a locked test set still provides an honest final sign-off after many iterative decisions.
items.example: Ten algorithm changes ranked by 5-fold CV—reporting only the best CV mean without a untouched test set overstates true generalization.

items.myth: A higher k always gives a better estimate.
items.reality: Larger k uses more data per train fold but increases correlation between folds and compute; LOOCV can have high variance. Common choices are 5 or 10 for a bias–variance–cost balance.
items.example: LOOCV on n=200 logistic regression: mean accuracy 0.91 but fold scores swing 0.82–0.98; 5-fold mean 0.89 with std 0.02 is more stable to report.

items.myth: CV and train/val/test splits are unrelated ideas.
items.reality: CV is a protocol that rotates validation roles across folds; hold-out splits fix those roles once. Projects often CV on train+val and keep test locked.
items.example: 80/10/10 split: run 5-fold CV on the 90% train+val block; evaluate the chosen model once on the 10% test block.

items.myth: Cross-validation is only for preventing overfitting.
items.reality: CV is primarily an evaluation protocol for estimating out-of-sample performance; mitigation-focused uses (nested tuning, small-data regularization) share the same mechanics but different emphasis—see Overfitting Mitigation → Cross-Validation.
items.example: Reporting mean ROC-AUC from stratified 5-fold to compare two scorers is evaluation-first; using inner CV for early stopping is mitigation-first.
